segments.text: Yes.
Yeah.
But anyway, congratulations for that.
Hey, now, we've got a bit of time with you this morning.
Looking forward to hearing what you've got to say.
As a committee, as you know, we love asking questions.
I have become accustomed to that reality, yeah, Chair.
Are you comfortable if we just make some-
Mm
... for this occasion, very short remarks, just to try and frame-
Yeah
... the conversation and shape some of the questions.
So thanks, firstly, for inviting us to talk to you about the long-term insight
briefing.
Uh-huh.
And I think you also wanted to maybe spend a short amount of time on the mandamus
order.
Yes.
So-
We've got one member particularly interested.
So I just-
Everyone should be interested.
Draw your attention. If you need to talk about the legal situation-
Yep
... so be upfront.
Awesome. Yeah, so I'll cover that in my opening remarks just in terms of framing
it, because I am able to talk about it to a level, and then there are some
limitations.
But can I just make sure I've introduced my colleagues who are sitting at the
table? You are familiar, I think-
... we've got Juanita Ryan, deputy chief executive at the moment, Communities,
Partnerships, and Pathways. And Tom Baker, who's our chief
digital officer.
So they're both here.
I assume the chief digital officer's role here is not needing to be
explained. And Juanita obviously brings lots of insight
around the rehabilitative reintegrative services, where we think there's a good
opportunity, and also at the moment is covering the community partnerships and
pathways space where also technology has a quite significant
role to play.
Corrections' LTIB on potential technology changes
looks over the next 25 years and focuses on areas where
technology could potentially help to address issues that may arise in the
future. So very importantly, framing this, this is
future-focused, long-term in outlook to help us plan.
Technology's already an important part of our operations.
I think we've spoken in the past about corrections officers already utilizing
body-worn cameras. Full body scanners having been introduced at some of our
prison sites and what that's then enabled in terms of reducing
strip searching, enabling the same activity to be done in a
much less intrusive way, both for those in our care as well as the officers who
work in that environment. Other technologies, including virtual reality,
for training purposes, have been trialed successfully in some sites.
Prisons though, and I think this is the important point to set context, prisons
specifically are, by design and necessity, hardened
environments. Often built with steel-reinforced concrete, particularly in the high
security, maximum security environment.
And therefore introducing new technology,
particularly on existing assets, can be difficult and expensive, sometimes
making it hard to take full advantage of all available technology both
now and into the future. Despite that, we are focused on having a plan for
how that can be introduced progressively, taking advantage of technology where it
makes sense, but is also affordable.
The LTIB looked at three key areas where technology could be used in the
future to add value or improve efficiency or safety.
Those being in prisons, in the community, and as I've said, in the rehabilitation
or integration space.
And I thought that would be a helpful focus into those three areas because
that's kind of how we've thought about it in the LTIB.
Before I finish, just on the specifics of the mandamus order,
just again to help frame what I will be able to talk about rather than what
is still subject to proceedings,
I will just briefly talk on the mandamus order and the work that is underway as a
result.
It's important to note that the order arose during existing proceedings, and
while it's somewhat independent, I'm going to caveat some of my answers given the
wider matter is still sub judice. So I'll just be clear about where those two
things sit and where I want to maybe take a step back from the
specifics and give you context rather than answering any specific
questions. The order requires that I as chief executive am required to comply
with Section 70 of the Corrections Act, in the administration of
conditions, specifically of detention in units 12 and 13 at Auckland
Prison. That's the maximum security environment.
And the order arose during proceedings focused on two
units at Auckland Prison related to Section 70 of the
act, which provides the detail of the minimum entitlements for exercise.
In short, the judgment touched on several issues, including not consistently
meeting open-air requirement, often due to infrastructure constraints, the
conflation of exercise and time out of cell, and that inadequate resourcing was
not a lawful basis for denying minimum entitlements.
We have responded to this. So it was important that we
heard, understood, and then took action around the issuing of that
mandamus order.
Responding by increasing resourcing, looking at new approaches to unlock
regimes to meet the requirements of the order, and I can confirm that the
circumstances that gave rise to the order have not occurred since those changes
have been made.
So I'll give that as the frame to hopefully then enable as much of a
constructive conversation as possible whilst not traveling into the
license.
Is it subject to appeal? Is that what he's-
That's what I was wondering.
No. Oh, sorry. It is, and we are not appealing that.
So are you finished then?
Yes, I am.
Right. I'm going to go to Timothy Paul-
Can you clarify that?
... first.
Yeah.
To ask him questions.
Yeah. Can we just clarify that?
Because before, we did a bit of research about whether this was still
ongoing, and our understanding was it was Corrections,
was it applied for an appeal, and that was denied by the judge, and that now that
it just is settled. Right?
We're not appealing. The mandamus order-
Yeah
... stands, but it is still part of a wider,
I think the term is interlocutory,
set of matters that is still a live case in the High Court.
So that's the interrelationship between the two. There's an order.
That is something that we had to apply and
comply with from the end of last year, and I can talk to that.
Mm.
But I'm not going to talk about any of the wider implications of the case because
that would be sub judice.
Yeah. Okay.
So you go.
Yeah. So,
over the last two yearsThis committee in
interview briefings and during scrutiny week have
asked yourself and your leadership whether Corrections is meeting minimum
entitlements. And from my recollection, it was always
Lee Marshall would answer a lot of the minimum entitlement questions.
Every time, Corrections said to us
that,
"You guys were meeting the minimum entitlements." So it was a bit of a
shock to see the High Court order last year.
So what genuine assurances can
Corrections give to our committee that this is
not just going to be a slap on the wrist for Auckland Prison?
What are you doing to oversee the fulfillment of those entitlements in every
single prison? And what
will be the consequences for the operation of
Auckland Prison not meeting its statutory obligations?
Correct. Okay. Well, I'll,
I think, take a step back in terms of
the specifics of the mandamus order, which was related to units 12 and
13 of Auckland Prison rather than any
sort of wider findings. The context of those two units,
as you'll be aware, I think you visited that environment,
is that they are managing the most complex, violent
people in our system.
And within that context, we have put in
place regimes that try and prioritize what is in our guiding
principles of the Corrections Act under Section 6,
maintaining public safety as our primary and paramount consideration in
decision-making. What that meant for us
on a day-to-day operational basis prior to the mandamus order
was that we were very much wanting to and attempting
to achieve minimum entitlements, particularly the one-hour
access to fresh air every day.
And what we were discovering every day
was that the nature of the volatility of the
individuals and the care required to ensure we were not
inappropriately mixing prisoners in their
open-air space to avoid serious assaults and harm
of prisoner-on-prisoner, as much as the
potential for serious assaults to our officers, that we
were seeing incidents that then enabled us to
apply an exception to achieving minimum entitlements under 69 [2] B
and C, which are enabling us, for reasons of security
of
the prison operations being threatened or health and safety
of
any person in the prison being threatened, lawful reasons
for why we would, in those situations, not apply
minimum entitlements.
Our step back,
working through this case and responding to the order,
has been for us to confront what we, I think, had drifted into,
given the complexity of the people and our
desire to achieve that paramount consideration of public safety,
we had probably normalized as core regime routine in
those two units,
exceptions almost every day as a result of concerns of
threats to safety and health and safety.
What now, having engaged with the order and having reflected on the
judge's clear consideration and therefore issuing of something that is
obviously a very substantive position, is that we needed to take a
step back and say, "Well, what do we need to do to ensure that we can still
account for the safety of our staff, who do
incredibly difficult work every day with those most violent people in our system,
but still accounts for that as the norm?" Because that is
what that unit is achieving. And we need to
address the resourcing of people to enable us
to operate a regime that normally can achieve those minimum entitlements.
So that was the big change for us in mindset, that we weren't
operating on a default of, well, this is just the resourcing that we've
applied historically to running a maximum security environment.
We now need to think, well, what additional resources do we need?
So what I can confirm as a result of that ruling, that
order, we've invested $2 million per
year in additional frontline staff capability
specifically to those units. That has increased the amount of officers who are
available, increased the number of movements that can occur, has
anticipated the complexity of the interrelationships, and has made
a safe working operation that now properly sees
exception rather than default being threat to prisoner safety or
health and safety concerns every day.
So I can confirm that we are now recording every day with
absolute consistency the adherence to and
achievement of minimum entitlements.
And where that is not achieved, and I think it's important to note, because some
days it won't be, we have got really clear
documentation that 69 [2] B or C,
one or other, has applied but then justifies why, and
we're explicit that that is not as a result of us not adapting and
adjusting our staffing and operating regime.
And I think to your broad question of how can we be assured that doesn't apply
elsewhere,
this particular part of our network and our system is that
if you can imagine a triangle of highest risk, most complex right at the top,
this is right at the top of that
organizational construct. And therefore, I am assured, because we now have that
same record of minimum entitlement achievement across our network, that
this was the exception. This was the area where we had
prioritized as paramount requirement public safety,
and that had allowed us to drift into not saying,
actually, things have changed. The complexity and nature of the prisoners has
become more unpredictable, more violent, and we now need probably to
consider different resourcing to one where the operations weren't
kind of putting their hand up. We unfortunately needed this
orderTo allow us to properly go in and say, "Something's got to change.
We need to adopt a regime that allows us more normally to maintain those
minimum entitlements."
You got another question?
Well, I was keen to kind of-
Duncan Webb.
I'll just suck on that.
Yeah.
Just that last comment just made me really raise an eyebrow, which was, we
needed this order. You knew once it was drawn to your
attention, and it clearly was, prior to proceedings being filed,
you knew you weren't complying with the legal obligations.
Why did it
require a judge, a
hearing and a judge to say,
"Do what the law says,"
for the corrections to respond in the way it has?
If I may,
our view of our position was that we were lawfully applying the
intent of the act. I referenced the Section 6 principles, the
paramount consideration being public safety above all other things,
and therefore it did need to be tested.
And so from our perspective-
So your view, if I've got the case right, your view was
you were having resourcing constraints,
which meant you couldn't safely
deliver under Section 70, and that was justified.
Is that essentially, in a nutshell, your case?
That is a simplified version of how we
were operating the site. We had considered
the risks and the considerations of how we achieved safe
operations in that particular unit, those two units.
And our response was to ensure safety was the paramount
consideration, and how we then worked our way through that was
ensuring that we could confirm that first before anything else, and
that was the way in which officers
in the line of fire, so to speak, were considering their
ability to weigh on a case-by-case,
day-by-day basis, whether or not what they were experiencing with a
particular prisoner being particularly aggressive or particularly violent on that
day amounted to a justification for exception
two. And that just gave us the opportunity to reflect, having heard from a judge
how they'd interpreted the law.
Right.
Because there are exceptions in cases of emergency and so on to
not comply with-
And threats to safety of prisoners or staff-
Right
... and threats to health and safety of prisoners as well.
But what has been proven by your, the case and your actions is that
properly resourced, this was a systemic problem of
under-resourcing.
Mm.
Rather than
some ongoing emergency.
That's the question.
Well, I would
be happy to debate that in terms of, until we got that crystallized for
us, our proposition was we were
weighing two very complex and careful things about
what is the bar, what are the standards of 69 (2) B
and C in every single circumstance for every single prisoner in that
most complex space, and whether or not, and I think this is what I've
tried to convey, whether or not in each circumstance we
had drifted into a mode where we were
almost assuming, because of the people who we were managing there,
that every day we were going to
have a set of circumstances where there would be exceptions that would mean minimum
entitlements wouldn't be met.
From what I heard you say before,
my reading of what you said before, and it's interesting, the interpretation,
is that the way you were operating,
you
had interpreted the law to be
such that you could operate the way you were operating.
Correct.
Right.
Correct. That's exactly the point.
And so up to the point of the time that the judge came out with his/her
opinion-
Yep
it was at that point.
Because that's what I thought you were trying to say before.
It's exactly what I was trying to say. Thank you.
But now what you've said is now that you recognize, you obviously recognize the
decision, you're spending 2 million a year to try and rectify.
Correct.
Yeah. And I might-
I might make my question wrap into the long term, if that's helpful.
Yeah, please.
Because I appreciate the answer,
but this is not the first time that Corrections has been
challenged over the provision of minimum entitlements, because during COVID,
obviously, there was a crisis that justified the reduction of meeting those
targets.
Yep.
But when we moved out of lockdown and when it was safe to go back to normal,
Corrections were not, I think it was at Arohata Women's Prison,
were not meeting minimum entitlements.
So this is not the first time that this has come up, which is what makes
me wonder whether this is a system-wide issue.
And from my observations, I wonder whether-
Well, next question
... this is going to the long-term bit. Hang on, just please let me finish.
It seems to me that a lot of the resourcing is going towards increasing
capacity of our prisons and managing the increase in prison population,
which has increased by nearly 1,000 people during your tenure as Chief Executive of
Corrections, as opposed to the quality of
providing the basic minimum entitles
entitled to every prisoner, regardless of the severeness or
seriousness of their offending.
Yep.
What you are describing is the period
during and then at the end of COVID, where I think I
have come to this committee in its various forms and acknowledged and
talked about the weighing of public safety being paramount,
our need to ensure that we had sufficient staff who were able to then
undertake safe work as we were transitioning and experiencing high turnover,
the need to recruit as demand increased, and how we managed
those tensions across our network.
So what I would acknowledge is as a result of
context changing and our need to be able to account for ourselves absolutely
explicitly that we were achieving minimum entitlements, which
historically were just that we are.
For the previous period from 1995 until
2022/23, there was no explicit
documented position that was assuring everyone that
every single person was achieving every single minimum entitlement in every single
day, but for the exceptions. We are now in a position
whereCOVID and the period out
required us to be able to evidence that we were achieving those things.
And what we are now, this is where the technology kind of link comes in,
we are now in a mode where
we have the opportunity, starting in unit
12 and 13 at Auckland Prison, to start to
remove
the need for officers to be tied up using their own
documented approach to recording all of these things, and
try and prioritize their time being connected to those in
our care, rather than documenting those things.
So we are not in a place where it's a live thing yet.
But what we have seen in this work is
humans undertaking lots of documented record
keeping is not the most effective and efficient,
beneficial use of their time. Where technology can play a part,
potentially, is the use of CCTV camera footage,
the connection of that back into our systems, and that to become more of an
automated approach to that absolute, credible,
auditable, documented record, freeing up
officer time. So that's for us what I think extending
our existing technology to something that will help us in the
future reduce the administrative burden on our
officers, free them up to spend more time, and create an even stronger
evidence trail than
what we have today. Because it's a human-based system rather than a machine-based
system at the moment.
Can I just-- I want to go back to the member's original-
Yep
... question.
Yep.
And I think she's, the second time she's asked the question.
Yep.
What this committee wants to be assured-
Yep
is as of today-
Yep
... can you give assurance to the committee that,
the minimum standards or whatever in terms of care-
Minimum entitlements, yep.
Whatever way you do it, that's for you.
But the committee wants to be assured that you are meeting
those, and if you're not, we would love to know where you
have areas of concern and by when you would expect to
address it. I think that was what the member was originally asking.
If I-
So I can keep it short
... I'm paraphrasing, but.
I can keep it short and say, yes, we are achieving those.
And our assurance process for that is, we now have-
The data
... measures for every single minimum entitlement, for every single prisoner
at every single site, and that is being reported on a daily basis.
So what all I'm reflecting on in terms of then moving into the technology
is, that is a resource, administratively burdensome
part of an officer's day, which is important
that we can do from a accounting for ourselves and
demonstrating adherence. But technology in the future
has probably got a really important part to play in being able to automate
that part of what our responsibilities are, to then free back up officer
time, which I think is far more appropriately spent
engaging with those in our care, trying to get them on a more positive
pathway so they leave us better with brighter prospects.
So that's the link, if you like, between-
Right. Are we clear on-
... an important conversation and the alternative.
Okay, I'm going to go to a new line of questioning now to Mr. Todd Simpson.
Well, thank you. We'll kind of pick up because I think obviously you said
technology focus in the insights briefing, long-term insights briefing.
Yep.
Community prisons, rehabilitation.
I think we've covered prisons a bit.
But keen to understand a bit more what you're thinking in the rehabilitation space
because yeah, kind of what did you uncover in putting together the
briefing around how technology could help?
Because obviously,
yeah, we want to make sure that there is opportunities for people
that are in your care to get rehabilitation.
Yeah. So, I think
acknowledging in my setup that there are really
material barriers to just
introducing what we might all view as normal technology into
a prison context, the hard infrastructure part.
We have
significant
pilot examples where the use of things like virtual reality,
which has been used particularly for the education part of
our services,
is a really valuable vehicle to drive engagement,
to drive connection to something beyond the institutional setting,
and can really give a lift to the educational
attainments that would otherwise
perhaps not be as beneficial. So I think our reflection is virtual
reality is a really valuable tool that's been particularly effective,
as say, in education and training, giving people access to some
certification, qualification that otherwise might not be as practical within a
prison context.
And it's shown real gains in terms of those things.
I'll turn to my colleagues who are probably much more knowledgeable on this, but I
would say what we have seen in other jurisdictions is a move
to then include more than education and training
within that virtual reality space.
And that, for me, is quite an interesting area to watch because
at a human level, I can perhaps appreciate with what is possible
now with virtual reality, the opportunity to move people out of
what they would have as their day-to-day world into something very different, which
might be super helpful in helping them shift their behaviors
and mindset.
So that might be an interesting area to focus on, that Juanita might have a view,
and then Tom from a delivery perspective.
And then the second bit is secure online learning and the ability
to ensure that we are
keeping abreast with how training and education is being
delivered with those normal polytech type
courses. And it's been critical for us to be able to shift our mode to
enable more and more access of an online nature rather than what has
historically been necessary to be our model, face-to-face
deliveryAnd that is becoming less and less the mode for some of that
delivery. But I don't know whether you've got any reflections, Juanita, around
virtual reality, broadening those to other services,
what you might have as a perspective in terms of how that might work
with kind of evidence and practice in some of those services, because there'll be
pros and cons, right?
Yeah, there will be. And in terms of virtual reality within
the offense-focused rehabilitative context,
I'm actually not aware of how that is being
utilized overseas specifically.
But I do know virtual reality more widely for mental health services for
some forms of specific
illnesses that people might have, has been trialed
from probably the late '90s in New Zealand around how we support people to
overcome some of those within kind of real-time or real-looking
contexts. So some of that would be particularly useful
for us to consider in a prison context, where it's also very challenging for people
to be in the real-life context.
And the relevance of that to us, I think, is that helps set people up for mental
health barriers that are really challenging them to engage in offense-focused rehab
proper.
As Jeremy said, in terms of virtual reality for education, we've had some really
good
trials of this within the Department of Corrections and with some really good
outcomes in terms of people being able to achieve some
NCEA-level results. Not across the
spectrum, or across the suite, or across the country,
but certainly something that we are seeing some real green shoots from.
Okay. So I'm going to Dr Tracy McLennan.
Thank you, Mr Chair. So I had a couple of questions, and Tom, you're probably
best placed to answer,
because the long-term briefing is really interesting.
No free lunches here, Tom.
But the briefing's really interesting, so I'm pleased that you're turning your mind
to all of the
potentials out there. But it's really important that the plans remain
adaptable, as you'll know, because
rather than being sort of locking in systems that are quickly going to be outdated.
So I'm assuming that that's forming part of your thinking as well.
So that's one question, and also noting that
the report talks about how new tech can change how crime is
detected and managed. So have you also considered whether
that sort of increased use of tech could itself
drive changes in prison demand over time?
I don't know if that was sufficiently sort of taken into account in
the briefing.
Okay, let's do those two. We're running out of time a bit.
Yeah. Look, my interactions with my peers and colleagues, not
just in New Zealand but in Australia, suggest that not really
in reduction of crime. However, there is a view
around how we could detect
just certain interactions between individuals within the custodial environment,
and then what could come about as a result of the data that then falls out of that,
that we could then leverage
for prisons operations perspective.
Because you can imagine a situation where internal charges, that changes the nature
of
potentially what you're monitoring, isn't it?
Yeah.
Yeah, absolutely.
Do you just want to touch on, I think the first question was sort of pushing
into the how do you maintain progress,
long-term view of where you're trying to get to without what you're doing becoming
outdated before
you've even done it. Did I feel some of that in your question?
Yeah.
Because I think that sort of speaks to the three phases-
Yeah
... of your digital SOI.
Yeah. So our focus in our digital statement intent is about
strengthening, enhancing, and transforming.
And so what we're attempting to do with that is stay
connected to, again, my peers, both here and not
just in the public sector but the private sector, but also across the ditch in
Australia. So on behalf of Corrections, I chair the emerging
technologies group of people that sit across the
corrections environment, and we meet quite regularly every quarter.
And the things that we talk about and discuss is the emergence of technology
and the longevity of that in terms of its use within our
environment. So we focus on particular areas.
Topics that we've touched on, virtual reality, we have talked about that in the
education space. That has limited time.
We've talked about AI, it seems the topic du jour
of the day, and we know that that in and of itself will have limited time span
as well. So we're trying to think beyond that.
What are the next things that come along that we might have to turn our
minds to in terms of supporting our particular function in
our environment?
Okay. And I think
about our integration offender management system, which is
our core platform where data exists to give us
information about every single person in our care, case noting, ability to
search, document.
That's a legacy system, an old system which has been maintained over
time. We're part of a wider justice system, as you well know.
So the interrelationship between a police system, a justice system, and a
corrections system that need to seamlessly interact
but are at different stages creates a sort of similar tension for us
in terms of when do we start thinking about what might be
next as a different legacy system, versus what do we need to do today
to maintain, stabilize, but sort of improve and enhance the
existing system. So I think IOMS is a really good example of how
we get confidence and assurance that we have a stable
platform whilst Te Reo Neka is
being introduced as the case management system within the courts part of justice,
so that we are ready with a stable platform to then start
to consider how we might shape our future systems
as that starts to come in to perhaps think about, and I think this
is a really important principle that we've used as a
department. We have to know that we have been
really disciplined in the way in which we articulate the operational
activities that we want to happen in a particular way, the
workflow-Before we start ambitiously
describing what the technology needs to be.
Mm.
So until we describe the workflow activities that
will help that work achieve our outcomes, we're not yet ready for the
technology.
So we're putting the effort into that-
Mm
... whilst Te Aureka is coming on, so that we're in a position to potentially
invest in some of these technologies.
We're running out of time. We're actually over time.
I'm going to have two more questions.
Sorry.
One more from Tracy, and then I'm going-
Very briefly.
Yep.
So I, and that-
Last one
... that largely answered it.
Cool.
So I assume making sure that you're able to measure and monitor-
Correct
... so that it's in service of rehab and-
Correct
... to reduce re-offending, not just throughput.
Correct.
Yep.
Correct.
Okay. Tama Sopor. Last one.
All right. So long term, looking at the makeup of the prison
population-
Yep
... accepting that you don't make the law,
there has been-
All the decisions that come into our agency.
Yeah.
Yep.
Yep. Accepting all of that. The proportion of Māori in prison is
still
worryingly high.
It seems that attempts or efforts to refresh Hōkai Rangi,
despite this committee recommending that, have not gone anywhere, and I'd love to
hear an update if I'm wrong on that.
There was a decision made to
remove the Māori Leadership Advisory Board to your leadership,
and
then to look at the woman's prison population.
That's shot up recently.
Under your leadership, there have been the Waikeria
riots, the high-profile escape of Phillip Smith,
and other really serious incidents, and incidences of
escapes resulting in innocent members of the public being harmed.
Okay, this is long-term insights. So long-term insights.
So let's-
Long-term insight, how are you going to change all of that?
Because it's not a great record.
Okay.
I am-
That's not even touching on the internal allegations of bullying and internal
cultures that is in the media right now.
Yeah. That's lots of
questions. My short answer to that is,
we work in a very complex part of the wider
system of public services. I am incredibly proud of
the work that everyone undertakes every day within a corrections context.
We are but one important part of a whole heap of different
agencies and parties that together leads to the
consequences that you have described.
We have been committed to ensuring that we are confronting some
of the realities in our part of the system.
You mentioned the bullying matters, and I'll reference our approach to
speaking up, standing up, sexual harassment being an absolute focus for us,
and over 2,300 people within our organization have already been
through really extensive training, not because we've told them they must,
but because they have determined that it's the right thing to do as a result of us
pushing in and saying, "It's incredibly important for us
more than anyone else to lead well and to be very conscious
of decency and dignity in all of our work." So I'd just
park that particular point in there.
Yeah.
And I think to your questions around Hōkai Rangi, addressing the
overrepresentation of Māori-
Mm
... and particularly around women,
it is a long-term intractable challenge-
Sure
... that is clearly not a corrections alone issue.

It incorporated words from many cultures and adopted to new
environments, was carried and reshaped by diverse people.
So English is fundamentally a language of movement, exchange, and
integration.
However, there's another point I want to pick up on.
And that's what the last speaker was starting to say.
As I was writing my submission, it became quite clear that the proposal to
make English an official language must also be considered in a broader
environment. Many voters, including myself, are not simply
evaluating your proposals, but we're also thinking about why this
issue?
Why now? Especially with everything that's going on in the world.
At what cost to more pressing matters?
At what cost to my children?
What are the drivers behind prioritizing symbolic legislation such as this?
And these
concerns,
we know that they consume parliamentary time,
attention, they divert focus away from substantive policy
challenges and contribute to public cynicism about political
motive.
So the implications are quite clear.
Given its history, it's difficult to argue that English requires protection
through official status.
Its strength lies in adaptability and global reach, not in legislative
reinforcement. At the same time, elevating English formally
risks undermining efforts to revitalize Te Reo Maori, creates
tension with commitments under Te Tiriti o Waitangi, and reinforces
perceptions of exclusion.
Again, many voters like myself are increasingly aware that our
parliamentary process has been captured to delay,
distract, and derail meaningful progress for our
children.
So here we are.
Biculturalism and multiculturalism is what needs to be protected.
In this context, where public confidence and political priorities is
already strained, advancing symbolic legislation
of this kind risks reinforcing the view that parliamentary
process like this is not aligned with the needs of the
people it serves to represent.
Okay. Thank you. I've got a question from Dr.
Sunan.
Thank you. Good to see you again.
Thank you.
Something you mentioned in terms of English being essentially a
chimeric language, really struck with the fact do you see any
challenges or confusion around not specifying what kind
of English and people then debating on whose English is the
official English?
Most probably, but humans evolve, and so do languages and so do
cultures. So of course it's going to create those problems.
What is the official language?
Slang? Well,
everything.
It will create problems down the end, not just in the exclusionary factors.
Thank you.
All right. Hey, thank you for making the effort to come on behalf of
the next generation and speaking to us today.
Thank you.
Thank you.
Right. We've got our last submitter who is online.
Good afternoon.
Are you there?
Hi. You might need to unmute. Is that right?
I think she's muted.
Oh, cool.
Nice to see you.
Now, we've got five minutes. We've read your submission.
Maybe highlight and then we might have a question or two for you.
Oh, kia ora. Kia ora koutou. Can you see me? And can you hear?
Yes. Yes.
Ka pai.
Nei rā te mihi ki a koutou i tē wā. Ki te taha tokū
māmā, nō Ruatoki ahau, ko Tūhoe te iwi.
Ki te taha tokū pāpā, nō Rāhui Pokika ahau, ko Waikato te iwi.
So this is an individual submission.
But I just have to let you know, I'm a lecturer of education, primary
teaching. I'm also a new member to
the-Tertiary Education Union Council.
So
I'm with Te Wānanga o Aotearoa, but it's an individual
submission. Ka pai. I'm just going to get straight to it.
He mihi ana
kautai.
So I'm here to express my strong opposition to the English Language
Bill. Thank you for the opportunity to speak today.
This bill solves no problems, delivers no practical benefit,
and uses public money and parliamentary time on an issue that simply
doesn't exist. English already functions as a dominant
language of government,
law, education, and public life in Aotearoa.
It is the language of Parliament, the courts, the public service, and
almost all official communications.
Sorry, I'm probably going to not take a breath, just carry on reading.
There is no evidence of confusion, disadvantage, or administrative
difficulty arising from English not being explicitly named in
legislation. In fact, government officials have publicly stated
that legislating English would make no practical difference
to how the system operates. So we're here debating a bill
that changes nothing in practice, yet still requires policy
development, legal drafting, departmental analysis, and full
parliamentary processing. All of that comes at a real cost
to taxpayers.
At a time when public services are under pressure, when health, education,
housing, and justice all face significant
challenges, it is difficult to justify spending public money on
a bill that does not improve outcomes for anyone.
Beyond the waste of resources, there is a deeper concern.
Although the bill claims it
does not diminish the status of Te Reo Māori, many New Zealanders are worried
that the message it sends,
English is not endangered, it does not require protection.
Te Reo Māori, on the other hand, has required decades of
revitalization efforts supported by progressive governments.
By elevating English in statute,
when
it is already dominant
in official domains, this bill risks being used rhetorically
to push back against bilingualism or to question the presence of Te Reo
Māori in public spaces. Even if that is not the
intent, it may be the effect.
Hang on. Just move my notes up.
Sorry.
Legislation needs to strengthen social cohesion, not create new points of
tension. It should also respond to genuine public need.
There is no evidence that New Zealanders are calling for this bill.
There's no evidence of harm that it addresses, and there's no evidence that
it will improve clarity, efficiency, or fairness in
public administration. What it does do is divert time,
money, and attention away from areas where
legislative action could make a meaningful difference,
such as reinstating Te Ahu o te Reo for kaiako in schools
across Aotearoa. For these reasons, I respectfully urge the committee to
decline the progress
of the English Language Bill. E mihi ana ki a kautou i tēnei wā.
Kia ora.
Thank you
very much. Hey, look, I want to thank you for your submission.
We've heard a number of submissions, so I don't think you should take out of
it that we haven't listened to what you've said.
But I want to thank you for making a submission and being with us today.
Kia ora.
Okay.
Right.
Nice to see you, professor. Right.
We're going into private session
